children.name: Pharomachrus
children.description: The resplendent quetzals — arguably the most visually stunning birds of the Neotropics, with iridescent green plumage, crimson bellies, and extraordinarily long upper tail coverts that cascade like a serpent's tail. The males' elongated tail feathers were sacred to Mesoamerican civilisations, used as currency and ceremony by Aztec and Maya nobility. The word quetzal comes from the Nahuatl quetzalli, meaning 'precious feather'.

children.name: Pharomachrus mocinno
children.description: The most famous trogon, found in highland cloud forests from southern Mexico to western Panama. The male is a living jewel, with iridescent green head and body, a crimson breast, and tail streamers up to one metre long. It was sacred to the Maya and Aztec civilisations and is the national bird of Guatemala, lending its name to the country's currency.

children.name: Pharomachrus auriceps
children.description: Found in the humid montane forests of the Andes from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia. As its name suggests, the male has a shimmering golden-bronze head and bronzy-green upperparts with a red belly. It replaces the Resplendent Quetzal in the Andean cloud forests.

children.name: Pharomachrus antisianus
children.description: A poorly known quetzal of the Andean foothills from Venezuela to Bolivia. It is distinguished from the Golden-headed Quetzal by its more prominent crest and green (not bronzy) head. Its ecology remains little studied due to its remote cloud-forest habitat.

children.name: Pharomachrus pavoninus
children.description: A quetzal of the western and central Amazon basin, found in lowland humid forests from Colombia and Venezuela to Bolivia and Brazil. The male has a green head and upper breast, a red belly, and shorter tail streamers than other quetzals. It inhabits terra firme forests, feeding on fruit and occasionally small vertebrates.

children.name: Euptilotis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Eared Quetzal, which bridges the morphological gap between quetzals and typical trogons. It has a distinctive tuft of feathers on each side of the head that resemble ears, giving it its common name. It is the only trogonid found in the pine-oak forests of the Sierra Madre Occidental.

children.name: Euptilotis neoxenus
children.description: A striking trogonid of the pine-oak forests of Mexico's Sierra Madre Occidental, with iridescent green upperparts, a red belly, and a distinctive tuft of feathers on each side of the head. It is the northernmost quetzal and occasionally strays into Arizona and New Mexico. Its call is a series of loud, frog-like croaks.

children.name: Trogon
children.description: The largest and most widespread trogon genus, comprising over 20 species across the Neotropics. Trogons are typically green or blue above with a contrasting red, orange, or yellow belly, and share a characteristic habit of sitting motionless on a perch before sallying out to catch insects or fruit. They nest in cavities excavated in rotting wood or termite mounds.

children.name: Trogon elegans
children.description: The most northerly-distributed trogon, found from the south-western United States (Arizona, New Mexico, Texas) through Mexico to Costa Rica. The male has a green head and chest, blue-green back, and coral-red belly and undertail. It inhabits dry canyons and riparian woodlands, where its slow, repeated call is a characteristic sound.

children.name: Trogon collaris
children.description: A widespread and variable trogon of humid lowland and montane forests from southern Mexico through the Amazon basin. The male has a green head and breast with a distinctive white band separating the breast from the red belly. Females are brown with a similar collar pattern.

children.name: Trogon personatus
children.description: A montane species found in cloud forests from Colombia and Venezuela south to Bolivia. The male has a green head and upper breast, a distinct white eye-ring, and a bright red belly. It is most easily distinguished from the similar Collared Trogon by its more restricted white chest band and the absence of barring on the undertail.

children.name: Trogon mexicanus
children.description: A highland trogon of montane pine-oak and cloud forests from Mexico to Honduras. The male has a metallic green head and upper breast, a blue-green back, and a yellow belly unlike the red-bellied trogons of the lowlands. Its slow, repeated, far-carrying call is a characteristic sound of Central American highland forests.

children.name: Trogon melanurus
children.description: A large, robust trogon of lowland humid forests from Panama through the Amazon basin. The male has a dark blue head and upper breast, a green back, a red belly, and a black tail with fine white barring. It is the only trogon with an entirely black tail from below.

children.name: Trogon chionurus
children.description: A striking trogon of lowland forests from Panama to western Ecuador and north-western Colombia, with a metallic green head and chest, a blue-green back, a bright yellow belly, and a white undertail. It was formerly considered conspecific with the Green-backed Trogon but is distinguished by its white undertail and different vocalisations.

children.name: Trogon viridis
children.description: A large, brightly coloured trogon of lowland forests in the Amazon and Guiana Shield regions. The male has a metallic blue head and chest, bright green back, yellow belly, and a black-and-white barred tail. It is one of the most common and conspicuous trogons in the Amazon basin, often seen perched motionless in the mid-storey.

children.name: Trogon caligatus
children.description: A small trogon of lowland humid forests from southern Mexico through Central America to north-western South America. The male has a metallic green head and upper breast, a blue-green back, a yellow belly, and a white undertail with broad black bars. It was formerly treated as a subspecies of the Violaceous Trogon.

children.name: Trogon ramonianus
children.description: A small trogon of the Amazon basin, recently split from the Violaceous Trogon complex. The male has a metallic green head and breast, a blue-green back, a yellow belly, and a white undertail with fine black barring. It inhabits lowland humid forests and forest edges, often perching in the mid-storey.

children.name: Trogon curucui
children.description: A beautiful trogon of dry and humid forests in south-central South America, from eastern Brazil to Bolivia, Paraguay, and northern Argentina. The male has a metallic blue crown and nape, a green back, a red belly, and a black-and-white barred tail. Its song is a series of slow, melancholy notes.

children.name: Trogon surrucura
children.description: A striking trogon of the Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil, Paraguay, and northern Argentina. The male has a turquoise-blue head and upper breast, a green back, a red belly, and orange-red eye-ring. The female has a grey head and breast with a white belly. Its song is a series of slow, descending whistles.

children.name: Trogon chrysochloros
children.description: A spectacular trogon of the Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil, with a metallic green head, black throat, blue-green back, bright yellow belly, and a white undertail. It was recently split from the Black-throated Trogon complex. It inhabits lowland and montane forests, often seen perched quietly in the mid-storey.

children.name: Trogon violaceus
children.description: A small trogon of the Guiana Shield region, with a metallic violet-blue head and upper breast, a green back, a yellow belly, and a white undertail with black barring. It inhabits lowland humid forests and forest edges, often perching in the canopy. Its song is a series of short, emphatic notes.

children.name: Priotelus
children.description: A small genus of trogons endemic to the Caribbean, with two species restricted to Cuba and Haiti. Their plumage is greener and less iridescent than mainland trogons, with distinctive white-tipped tail feathers.

children.name: Priotelus temnurus
children.description: The national bird of Cuba, endemic to the island where it is known as the tocororo. It has a green back, grey breast, red belly, and striking white-tipped tail feathers. Its three-note call is said to sound like 'tocoro-ro', and its blue-white-red plumage mirrors the colours of the Cuban flag.

children.name: Apaloderma
children.description: The only trogon genus found outside the Americas, with three species distributed across sub-Saharan Africa. African trogons are more arboreal and forest-dependent than their Neotropical counterparts, and are among the most elusive and poorly studied birds on the continent.

children.name: Apaloderma narina
children.description: The most widespread African trogon, found from West Africa through the Congo Basin to East Africa and south to South Africa. The male has a brilliant metallic green head and upper breast, crimson-red underparts, and a striking bare patch of blue-green skin around the eye. Named after Narina, the lover of the French ornithologist François Le Vaillant, it is a shy inhabitant of dense forest canopy.
